Psalm 106 is a painful mirror held up to God's people—and to us. The psalmist begins with genuine praise: the LORD is good, His love endures forever, and those who practice justice are blessed. But then something shifts. The prayer becomes a confession, and we watch Israel's entire story unfold as a catalog of rebellion. From Egypt's wonders to the Red Sea's deliverance, the people witness God's power firsthand, yet within moments they forget. They crave, they test, they envy their leaders, they exchange their Glory for a carved calf, they despise the land God promised them. The pattern repeats with devastating monotony: God acts, Israel forgets; God rescues, Israel rebels; God's anger burns, His people sink deeper into iniquity. What strikes hardest is the specificity—the earth swallowing Dathan, the fire consuming the wicked, parents sacrificing their own children to demons. This is not abstract sin. This is what forgetting God actually looks like in human history.

Yet something else happens in this psalm that we dare not miss. God does not simply erase this people for their rebellion. Instead, we see interruptions of mercy that break the cycle of judgment. Moses stands in the breach, and God's wrath is turned aside. Phinehas rises up to stop the plague, and his act is credited as righteousness. Most crucially, despite Israel's repeated failures and God's legitimate anger, He hears their cry. He does not forget His covenant. He remembers His loving devotion and relents. The psalmist is not excusing Israel's sin—the confession is sharp and unflinching—but he is testifying to something that outlasts human rebellion: the steadfast love of the LORD.

This psalm forces us to ask uncomfortable questions about ourselves. We also receive God's mercies. We also forget what He has done for us. We also turn to false promises and chase empty things while the true God waits for our attention. We also rationalize our disobedience and resist His voice. The difference between Israel's history and our own may be less significant than we assume. But here is where the psalm offers not cheap comfort but real hope: the same God who did not abandon Israel despite her repeated breaking of covenant is the God we serve. His covenant now stands in Christ, sealed by blood that speaks louder than all our failures. This psalm asks us to see our own capacity for rebellion clearly, to stop pretending we are better than those who came before, and then to run to the God whose loving devotion truly endures forever.

The closing verses pull us back to where we started: "Save us, O LORD our God, and gather us from the nations, that we may give thanks to Your holy name." The psalmist's prayer is not for escape from consequences but for restoration to worship. After rehearsing all that Israel has done wrong, after confessing our own complicity in that same pattern of forgetting, the only fitting response is to cry out for salvation and to pledge ourselves to gratitude. The psalm ends not in despair but in benediction—blessing the LORD from everlasting to everlasting. This is what it means to truly encounter God's mercy: not to forget what you have done, but to remember what He has done, and to let that remembrance become the fuel for praise.
